content:
You are a creative and professional JavaScript game developer who builds polished and visually appealing video games using p5.js primitives.
Your game should offer a fun and engaging game experience for the player following game development principles.
Create a learnable progression ramp that balances learning, fun, and challenge.
Use your creativity to enrich the concept into an innovative design before coding, adding interesting and consistent elements beyond the game concept.
Ensure the implementation is free of inconsistencies and errors. Write modular ES6 code with automated tests covering key aspects of the game.
Follow the hard constraints exactly; violating any is a critical error.

<hard_constraints>
- The full game can be expanded by adding more elements to the game concept but should align with the game concept respecting the hard constraints and game design principles.
- Game must load and function without errors, start on pressing ENTER, gameplay must be responsive to player inputs, and have correct game phase handling.
- Game phases: START→PLAYING (ENTER), PLAYING→GAME_OVER (win/lose), GAME_OVER→START (R), PLAYING↔PAUSED (ESC). Expose this in a variable called `gameState.gamePhase` where `gameState` is a global object accessible via `window.getGameState()`.
- Start screen should have a title, short description of the game, instructions to play the game, and a "PRESS ENTER TO START" prompt.
- Allowed libraries: p5.js, p5.collide2D
- Canvas: `600 × 400 px`; target 60 FPS
- Keyboard inputs only. No mouse control.
- Allowed gameplay control keys: Arrow keys (37-40), Space (32), Shift (16), Z (90)
- Game phase specific controls:
    - ENTER (13)  – start the game
    - ESC (27)    – pause / unpause
    - R (82)      – restart; returns to the start screen, where the player can press ENTER to play again
- No external images, sprites, fonts, or other assets. Make visual assets and animations using code.
- No audio or sound effects.
- Use p5.js in instance mode and expose the game instance globally as window.gameInstance.
- Ensure game reproducibility using `p.randomSeed(42)` in the `setup` function. No other random seeding.
- Use ES6 syntax with proper imports and exports at the top of the file where they are used. No dynamic imports or require() imports.
- Maintain `p.logs` as write-only; log game_info, inputs, player_info with proper initialization and updates during the game loop.
</hard_constraints>

<instructions>
<game_concept_to_game_design>
Expand the game concept with new game elements with a well-planned progression and learning curve that enables the player to learn about the game elements and mechanics.
For inspiration, following are some examples of game elements that you can add to the game design as long as the final game satisfies the requirements of the game concept:
- environment: game setting, genre, viewpoint
- entities: characters, opponents, collaborators, NPCs, objects in the game, power-ups, destructible objects, obstacles
- mechanics: rules of the games, final win conditions, game over conditions, subgoals, rewards, player actions and abilities, interaction between entities
- graphics: visual appearance and animations of the game entities and the environment
</game_concept_to_game_design>

<code_instructions>
You are encouraged to write as much code as you can to make a fun game, aesthetically appealing, and provide a fun gameplay experience for the player when playing the game for the first time.

<code_organization>
- Modular code organization with proper ES6 imports:
  - Organize the code into multiple files as needed (game.js, globals.js, etc.)
  - Group related functionality together in meaningful modules
  - Proper imports:
    - Use ES6 for imports and exports
    - Every file MUST import ALL constants, variables, functions, classes, and external symbols at the top of every file. No Node.js style require imports. No dynamic imports within functions
```javascript
// Essential pattern (ALWAYS follow this)
import { Player, Enemy } from './entities.js';
import { checkCollision, applyPhysics } from './physics.js';
import { gameState, CANVAS_WIDTH } from './globals.js';

// Example function with proper dependencies
function createPlayer(x, y) {
  const player = new Player(x, y);
  gameState.player = player;
  gameState.entities.push(player);
  return player;
}
```

- State Management:
  - Implement `function getGameState()` and attach it to `window`. It must return the `gameState` object.
  - Use a single `gameState` object to track the game's current data and status. Store all game data in the `gameState` object to be used by automated testing code to play-test the game.    
```javascript
export const gameState = {
  player: null,       // player entity to be initialized in the setup function and initialized before the game starts
  entities: [],       // all game entities including player
  score: 0,
  gamePhase: "START", // "START", "PLAYING", "GAME_OVER_WIN/LOSE", "PAUSED"
  controlMode: "HUMAN", // "HUMAN", "TEST_1", "TEST_2", "TEST_3", etc.
  ... // other game state variables which are needed to be tracked for automated testing
};
```
</code_organization>

<p5_instructions>
- Use p5.js in instance mode and store the p5 instance in a variable called `gameInstance`. Expose the game instance globally as follows:
```javascript
const p5 = window.p5
let gameInstance = new p5(p => {
    // Initialize variables
    ...
    // Initialize the logs. Important: Do not reset the logs at any point in the code! logs are considered write-only!
    p.logs = {
        "game_info": [],  // Information about the game
        "inputs": [],     // Information about the key inputs
        "player_info": [] // Information about the player character
    };

    // Functions
    ...
});
// Expose the game instance globally
window.gameInstance = gameInstance;
```
- p.logs is write-only. Do not reset it. We want to keep track of all the logs since the start of the p5.js game instance.
- Variables to store in the p.logs during gameplay:
  - In "game_info": Information about the game when there are changes in the game phases
    - "data": Any data like game phase or anything else you want to log
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event accessed using `p.frameCount` and `Date.now()` respectively
  - In "inputs": Store the control inputs when they are triggered by the player
    - "input_type": The input event type (keyPressed, keyReleased, etc.)
    - "data": Additional data specific to the input type. Store `key` and `keyCode` if the input is a key.
    - "framecount" and "timestamp": The framecount and timestamp of the event
  - In "player_info": Information about the player character when there are changes in the player's state (accessed via `gameState.player` where `gameState` is accessible via `window.getGameState()`)
    - "screen_x", "screen_y": The x and y position of the player on the screen
    - "game_x", "game_y": The x and y position of the player in the game world
    - "framecount": The framecount of the event

- Useful Functions:
  - Use p. to call p5 functions.
  - p5: `keyIsDown(key_code)`, `noLoop()/loop()`, `tint()`, `push()/pop()`, `textAlign()`, `textSize()`, `text()`
  - p5.collide2d for collision detection
    - Note that the specific order of the shapes in the function name matters. For example, 'collideCircleRect' is not available.
    - Available functions: `collidePointPoint`, `collidePointCircle`, `collidePointEllipse`, `collidePointRect`, `collidePointLine`, `collidePointArc`, `collideRectRect`, `collideCircleCircle`, `collideRectCircle`, `collideLineLine`, `collideLineCircle`, `collideLineRect`, `collidePointPoly`, `collideCirclePoly`, `collideRectPoly`, `collideLinePoly`, `collidePolyPoly`, `collidePointTriangle`
</p5_instructions>

<game_mechanics_instructions>
Implement intuitive, believable mechanics that enable players to achieve the final goal.
Choose parameters and logic for game mechanics that are intuitive and believable. These can change as the game progresses. 
Even at the end of the game, the mechanics must be such that the player can win by showing their learned skill.
- Controls:
  - Player controls must be intuitive and impact the game state in a meaningful way
  - Input handling: Use keyboard for HUMAN mode, automated testing action outputs for automated testing modes
  - When automated testing mode is selected, the game must automatically play using the action outputs from the automated testing controller
- Non-player characters and objects:
  - NPCs and objects must be spawned and behave respecting the progression of the game
  - Implement intelligent, intuitive, and challenging behavior for all game entities to keep the player engaged
- Interactions with other entities and objects:
  - Have plausible physics with parameters and logic to give it a believable realistic feel
  - Prevent objects from passing through each other or behaving inconsistently to prevent shocking the player
- GAME_OVER Conditions:
  - Implement mechanics that can lead to win or lose conditions achievable by the player
  - GAME_OVER must be a final state followed by game over screen
- Rewards:
  - Define meaningful and reasonable conditions for rewards to keep the player motivated to reach the final goal
  - Do not excessively repeat the same rewards
</game_mechanics_instructions>

<rendering_instructions>
- Implement aesthetic objects, smooth animations, and dynamic effects for the game environment, entities, and their interactions
- Provide useful visual feedback:
  - Player character must be distinctly identifiable
  - Provide visual feedback for interactions and response to their actions
  - UI: Add well-positioned text and graphics to inform the player about information like score, health, etc.
  - Never display current controlMode (HUMAN/TESTING) on the game canvas.
- Ensure the correct render order of the game objects to ensure player is visible on the screen
- Avoid flickering:
  - Do not call p.randomSeed() inside draw()
  - Have exactly one call to p.background() at the top of draw()
  - Do not draw elements that are randomly sampled at every frame as this causes flickering
  - Use ternary operator to set colors for conditional rendering: Example: `p.fill(...(CONDITION ? [255, 220, 150] : [40, 30, 20]));`
- Required Informative Game Screens:
  - Start: Title, clear instructions, objectives, and controls with actions for the player, "PRESS ENTER TO START"
  - Playing: Game rendering, score, and active gameplay
  - Pause: Small text "PAUSED" indicator in top right
  - Game Over: Win/Lose message, final score, "PRESS R TO RESTART", R takes you back to the start screen
</rendering_instructions>

</code_instructions>
</instructions>


<task>
Implement a complete, fun, and error-free p5.js game for the following concept:
{
  "original_game_name": "Mots Fl\u00e9ch\u00e9s Pro: Mots Crois\u00e9s",
  "game_name": "Arrow Words Challenge",
  "genre": "Puzzle, Word",
  "core_mechanics": "Players interact with a grid of cells to complete words. The primary mechanic involves selecting an empty cell, which highlights a specific word path (horizontal or vertical, determined by an embedded arrow clue). Players then use a keyboard to input letters one by one into the selected cells. Inputting a letter automatically advances the cursor to the next empty cell in the path. If the inputted letter is correct, it remains; if incorrect, it might be visually flagged or simply not advance. The game system validates words as they are completed or when all letters in a path are filled. The objective is to correctly fill all word paths in the grid. A hint system, 'Reveal Letter,' allows players to uncover a single correct letter in a selected word path, at a points cost. Game state tracked includes: the current grid (player's entries), the solution grid (for validation), the currently selected cell's coordinates, the list of cells comprising the currently highlighted word path, the elapsed time for the puzzle, the player's current score, the number of available hints, the current level ID, and the game's overall status (playing, paused, etc.).",
  "visual_elements": "The game's primary visual is a square **Puzzle Grid** composed of individual cells. \n\n**Cell Types:**\n-   **Empty Cell:** A square with a light background (e.g., white) and a black border, awaiting a letter.\n-   **Filled Cell:** An empty cell that now contains a single, large, clear, easy-to-read uppercase letter (e.g., Arial, 24pt, black).\n-   **Clue Cell:** A square with a distinct background color (e.g., light grey) and a dark grey border. It contains smaller, clear clue text (e.g., Arial, 12pt, black) and a small arrow icon (black triangle) indicating the word's direction (right or down).\n-   **Blocked Cell:** A square with a dark background (e.g., dark grey or black), indicating an impassable space where no letters can be placed.\n\n**Interaction Visuals:**\n-   **Selected Cell:** The currently active cell where input will go, highlighted with a thicker, distinct border (e.g., blue).\n-   **Highlighted Word Path:** When a cell is selected, all empty and filled cells belonging to its associated word path (as indicated by the clue's arrow) are highlighted with a distinct background color (e.g., light yellow).\n\n**UI Elements:**\n-   **Score Display:** 'Score: XXXX' text, prominently displayed in the top-right corner, in a clear font (e.g., Arial, 18pt, black).\n-   **Time Display:** 'Time: MM:SS' text, displayed in the top-left corner, similar font to score.\n-   **Level Indicator:** 'Level X' text, displayed top-center.\n-   **Hint Button:** A clearly labeled button (e.g., 'Hint (50 Pts)') positioned in the bottom-right, indicating cost and count if applicable.\n-   **Pause Button:** A small icon or button (e.g., '||') near the score display.\n-   **Win/Level Complete Screen:** A full-screen overlay with text like 'Level X Complete!', showing final score, and 'Next Level' and 'Main Menu' buttons.\n-   **Menu Screen:** Contains the game title, 'Play', 'Instructions', and 'Credits' buttons.\n\n**Animations/Effects:**\n-   **Cell Selection:** Instantaneous background and border color changes for selected cell and highlighted path.\n-   **Correct Letter:** A subtle, brief green flash or pulse on a cell when a correct letter is placed.\n-   **Incorrect Letter:** A brief red flash on a cell when an incorrect letter is attempted, then reverts.\n-   **Word Completion:** A temporary green highlight across an entire word path when it is fully and correctly completed.\n-   **Level Transition:** A fade-to-black effect followed by a fade-in of the new level or transition screen.\n-   **Star Earning:** (Simplified) Visual feedback on the win screen confirming level completion and score.",
  "control_scheme": "The game primarily relies on keyboard input for letter entry and navigation, supplemented by mouse clicks for initial cell selection and UI interaction.\n\n-   **Mouse Click (Left Button):**\n    -   On an empty or filled grid cell: Selects that cell, highlights its associated word path (based on the nearest clue's arrow), and sets it as the active input target.\n    -   On UI buttons (e.g., 'Play', 'Hint', 'Next Level', 'Pause'): Triggers the corresponding action.\n-   **Alphanumeric Keys (A-Z):**\n    -   When a grid cell is selected: Inputs the pressed letter into the selected cell. Automatically moves the selection cursor to the next empty cell in the highlighted word path. Input is case-insensitive, treated as uppercase.\n-   **Backspace / Delete:**\n    -   When a grid cell is selected: Clears the letter from the selected cell. If the cell was already empty, moves the selection cursor to the previous cell in the highlighted word path.\n-   **ArrowLeft / ArrowRight / ArrowUp / ArrowDown:**\n    -   When a grid cell is selected: Moves the selection cursor one cell in the respective direction. If the cursor is moved off the current word path, it selects the cell in the new position and highlights its associated word path.\n    -   If at the end/beginning of a word path, navigation attempts to wrap around or find the nearest valid cell in the grid.\n-   **Space:**\n    -   When a grid cell is selected: Moves the selection cursor to the next empty cell within the *currently highlighted* word path, regardless of its position relative to the current cell.\n-   **Enter:**\n    -   When a grid cell is selected: Triggers an immediate validation check for the entire currently highlighted word path. If correct, the word permanently locks in; if incorrect, no change (visual feedback might be provided).\n-   **Escape:**\n    -   During `PLAYING` state: Toggles the `PAUSED` state.",
  "game_states": "The game will transition through the following states:\n\n1.  **`MENU`**:\n    -   **Description:** The initial state, displaying the game title ('Arrow Words Challenge') and main navigation options.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`PLAYING`:** Triggered by clicking the 'Play' button. Loads Level 1.\n        -   **`INSTRUCTIONS`:** Triggered by clicking the 'Instructions' button.\n        -   **`CREDITS`:** Triggered by clicking the 'Credits' button.\n\n2.  **`INSTRUCTIONS`**:\n    -   **Description:** Displays a screen explaining how to play the game.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`MENU`:** Triggered by clicking a 'Back' or 'Main Menu' button.\n\n3.  **`CREDITS`**:\n    -   **Description:** Displays information about the game's creators.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`MENU`:** Triggered by clicking a 'Back' or 'Main Menu' button.\n\n4.  **`PLAYING`**:\n    -   **Description:** The active gameplay state where the puzzle grid is displayed, letters are input, and the game timer runs. Score, time, and level indicators are visible.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`PAUSED`:** Triggered by clicking the 'Pause' button or pressing 'Escape'.\n        -   **`WIN_LEVEL`:** Triggered when all word paths in the current level's puzzle are correctly completed.\n        -   **`GAME_OVER`:** Triggered by explicitly choosing to 'Give Up' or 'Exit to Main Menu' from the pause menu before completing the level.\n\n5.  **`PAUSED`**:\n    -   **Description:** The game loop is frozen. An overlay displays 'Paused' and options.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`PLAYING`:** Triggered by clicking 'Resume' button.\n        -   **`PLAYING` (Restart Current Level):** Triggered by clicking 'Restart Level' button (resets score, timer, and grid for current level).\n        -   **`MENU`:** Triggered by clicking 'Main Menu' button.\n\n6.  **`WIN_LEVEL`**:\n    -   **Description:** Displayed upon successful completion of a puzzle. Shows 'Level X Complete!', player's score for the level, and options.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`PLAYING` (Next Level):** Triggered by clicking 'Next Level' button. Loads the next level's puzzle.\n        -   **`MENU`:** Triggered by clicking 'Main Menu' button.\n\n7.  **`GAME_OVER`**:\n    -   **Description:** Displayed when a player quits a puzzle prematurely. Shows 'Game Over', final score for that attempt, and options.\n    -   **Transitions:**\n        -   **`PLAYING` (Restart Current Level):** Triggered by clicking 'Restart Level' button (resets score, timer, and grid for current level).\n        -   **`MENU`:** Triggered by clicking 'Main Menu' button.",
  "win_conditions": "The player wins the current level when all cells in the puzzle grid that are designated for letters are filled with their correct corresponding letters, thereby completing all word paths defined by the embedded arrow clues.",
  "lose_conditions": "The player 'loses' a specific puzzle attempt if they explicitly choose to exit the current puzzle (e.g., via a 'Give Up' or 'Exit to Main Menu' option from the pause screen or `PLAYING` state) before completing all words. There is no traditional 'game over' based on health or lives; failure is defined as non-completion.",
  "scoring_system": "Players earn points for progression and accuracy, with penalties for hints. The score is displayed prominently as 'Score: XXXX' in the top-right corner of the screen during gameplay and on win/lose screens.\n\n**Points Awarded:**\n-   **+10 points:** Per correct letter placed into an empty cell for the first time.\n-   **+100 points:** Per word path correctly completed (all its letters are correct and filled).\n-   **+500 points:** For successfully completing an entire level.\n-   **Time Bonus:** `+(maxLevelTimeSeconds - timeTakenSeconds) * 2` points, awarded upon level completion (if `timeTakenSeconds` is less than `maxLevelTimeSeconds`). This encourages faster completion.\n\n**Bonus Points:**\n-   **No Errors Bonus (Word):** `+50` points if a word is completed without any incorrect letters ever being typed into its path.\n-   **No Errors Bonus (Level):** `+500` points if the entire level is completed without any incorrect letters ever being typed into any cell.\n\n**Penalties:**\n-   **-50 points (base):** Per 'Reveal Letter' hint used. This cost can increase with difficulty levels.\n\n**High Score System:**\n-   A local high-score table will be maintained, storing the top 5 scores (Name, Score, Level Reached) using `localStorage`. Scores are updated upon level completion or game over.\n\n**Score Impact on Gameplay:**\n-   The hint system directly subtracts points, making their use a strategic decision impacting the final score. Higher scores contribute to the sense of progression, even without explicit badges or external leaderboards.",
  "level_system": "The game features a structured progression through 5 distinct levels, with difficulty increasing in measurable ways. Upon completing a level, a 'Level X Complete!' screen is displayed, and the player advances to the next level by clicking 'Next Level.' A 'Level X' indicator is shown at the top-center of the screen during gameplay.\n\n1.  **Level 1: The Basics**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Easy\n    -   **Grid Size:** Small (e.g., 6x6 cells).\n    -   **Word Lengths:** Predominantly short (3-5 letters).\n    -   **Number of Words:** Low (5-7 words).\n    -   **Complexity:** Simple, direct, common vocabulary clues. Minimal intersecting words. Abundant empty cells for easy navigation.\n    -   **Hint Cost:** -50 points.\n    -   **Max Level Time (for bonus):** 3 minutes.\n\n2.  **Level 2: Building Blocks**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Medium\n    -   **Grid Size:** Medium (e.g., 9x9 cells).\n    -   **Word Lengths:** Mixed (3-7 letters).\n    -   **Number of Words:** Moderate (8-12 words).\n    -   **Complexity:** More intersecting words, slightly longer clues, expanding vocabulary. Fewer empty cells than Level 1.\n    -   **Hint Cost:** -75 points.\n    -   **Max Level Time (for bonus):** 5 minutes.\n\n3.  **Level 3: Interconnections**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Hard\n    -   **Grid Size:** Large (e.g., 12x12 cells).\n    -   **Word Lengths:** Longer words become more common (5-9 letters).\n    -   **Number of Words:** Higher (12-18 words).\n    -   **Complexity:** Heavily intersecting words, denser grid, more challenging and nuanced clues, potentially less common vocabulary. Strategic placement of blocked cells.\n    -   **Hint Cost:** -100 points.\n    -   **Max Level Time (for bonus):** 8 minutes.\n\n4.  **Level 4: Lexical Labyrinth**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Very Hard\n    -   **Grid Size:** Very Large (e.g., 15x15 cells).\n    -   **Word Lengths:** Longer and more complex words (6-10+ letters).\n    -   **Number of Words:** Significant (18-25 words).\n    -   **Complexity:** Very dense grids with highly interconnected words. Clues require broader general knowledge and vocabulary. Fewer empty cells, almost a full grid.\n    -   **Hint Cost:** -125 points.\n    -   **Max Level Time (for bonus):** 12 minutes.\n\n5.  **Level 5: Master Architect**\n    -   **Difficulty:** Extreme\n    -   **Grid Size:** Expansive (e.g., 18x18 cells).\n    -   **Word Lengths:** Focus on very long, specific, and often specialized vocabulary words (8-12+ letters).\n    -   **Number of Words:** Very High (25-35 words).\n    -   **Complexity:** Extremely dense grids with intricate interdependencies. Clues are cryptic, thematic, and demand deep linguistic skill. Minimal empty cells.\n    -   **Hint Cost:** -150 points.\n    -   **Max Level Time (for bonus):** 18 minutes.",
  "simplifications": "-   **Level Count:** The original 'over 4000 free levels' is simplified to 5 distinct, pre-defined levels. Levels will be static puzzle definitions, not procedurally generated.\n-   **Progression/Badges:** The 'stars and badges, from Bronze to Legend' system is simplified to a score-based high-score table. No visual badge system or extensive progression tree beyond level completion.\n-   **Daily Bonuses:** 'Daily bonuses' and 'special rewards after five consecutive days' are removed, as they imply persistent user accounts and server-side tracking, which is out of scope for a basic HTML5 implementation.\n-   **Hint Types:** Only the 'Reveal Letter' hint is implemented. Other potential hint types (e.g., reveal word, check word) are excluded.\n-   **Mobile Focus:** While the original implies a mobile game, the HTML5 implementation prioritizes desktop controls (keyboard) over a virtual on-screen keyboard, although mouse clicks for selection are maintained.\n-   **Vocabulary/Linguistic Skills:** The conceptual benefit of increasing vocabulary is inherent to word puzzles but is not explicitly tracked or gamified within the implementation specifications.",
  "implementation_notes": "1.  **Technology Stack:** The game will be implemented using p5.js for all graphics rendering, user interface, and event handling. Matter.js is not suitable or necessary for this game's mechanics.\n2.  **Puzzle Data:** Each level's puzzle (grid layout, clue positions, clue text, and solution letters) will be stored in a structured format (e.g., JSON objects) that can be easily loaded by the p5.js sketch.\n3.  **Rendering Strategy:** Utilize p5.js's `createCanvas`, `background`, `rect`, `text`, and `image` (for arrows) functions. Dynamic calculation of cell sizes and positions will ensure basic responsiveness across different window sizes. Emphasis on `textFont` and `textSize` for excellent readability as per conversion notes.\n4.  **State Management:** A clear state machine approach using a global `gameState` variable will govern the overall flow of the game, dictating what is rendered and what logic is executed.\n5.  **Input Handling:** `mouseClicked()` for UI interaction and initial cell selection; `keyPressed()` for letter input and navigation (Arrow keys, Space, Backspace/Delete, Enter).\n6.  **Score Display:** The score will be rendered persistently in the top-right corner of the canvas using p5.js `text()` function, ensuring it's always visible during the `PLAYING` state.\n7.  **Level Progression:** A `currentLevelIndex` variable will track the player's progress. Upon `WIN_LEVEL`, this index increments, and the data for the next level is loaded. If the last level is completed, the game might revert to the `MENU` state or show a 'Game Complete' screen.\n8.  **High Scores:** `localStorage` will be used for persistent storage of high scores, including player name, score, and the highest level reached.\n9.  **No Matter.js:** Physics engine functionalities are not required for this grid-based word puzzle; therefore, Matter.js will not be included in the implementation."
}
</task>
<example_html>
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <meta charset="UTF-8" />
    <style>
      html, body { margin: 0; padding: 0; height: 100%; overflow: hidden; background: #222; }
      body { display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: center; align-items: center; }
      canvas { border: 1px solid #333; width: 600px !important; height: 400px !important; }
      .control-buttons { display: flex; gap: 10px; margin-bottom: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; justify-content: center; }
      .control-button { padding: 8px 16px; cursor: pointer; background: #444; color: #fff; border: none; border-radius: 4px; }
      .control-button.active { background: #007bff; }
    </style>
  </head>
  <body>
    <div style="text-align: center; margin-bottom: 20px;">
      <h1 id="gameTitle" style="color: #fff; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; margin-bottom: 10px;">{game_title}</h1>
      <div class="control-buttons">
        <button id="humanModeBtn" class="control-button active" onclick="window.setControlMode('HUMAN')">Human Mode</button>
        <button id="test_1_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_1')">TEST (Basic testing)</button>
        <button id="test_2_ModeBtn" class="control-button" onclick="window.setControlMode('TEST_2')">TEST (Win)</button>
      </div>
      <p id="gameDescription" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_description}</p>
      <p id="gameControls" style="color: #ccc; font-family: Arial, sans-serif; max-width: 600px; margin: 0 auto 20px auto; line-height: 1.4;">{game_controls}</p>
    </div>
    <script src="https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/ajax/libs/p5.js/1.4.0/p5.js"></script>
    <script src="https://unpkg.com/p5.collide2d@0.7.3/p5.collide2d.js"></script>
    <script type="module" src="game.js"></script>
  </body>
</html>

</example_html>

Arrow Words Challenge is a crossword-style puzzle game where you fill in a grid of words using letter clues embedded directly in the grid. Arrow Words Challenge

Arrow Words Challenge is a crossword-style puzzle game where you fill in a grid of words using letter clues embedded directly in the grid. Each clue has an arrow pointing right or down to indicate the direction of the word. Complete all words correctly to win the level. Use hints wisely as they cost points. Progress through 5 increasingly challenging levels, each with larger grids and more complex words. Earn points for correct letters, completed words, and fast completion times!

ENTER: Start game from menu | ESC: Pause/unpause game | R: Return to start screen | Arrow Keys: Navigate between cells in the grid | A-Z: Type letters into selected cells | BACKSPACE: Delete letter and move back | SPACE: Jump to next empty cell in current word | TAB: Use hint to reveal a letter (costs points)
